SF-101._scenario: Story — In Progress, high priority, with acceptance criteria and comments
SF-101.summary: Implement Stripe payment gateway integration for checkout flow
SF-101.description:
As a customer, I want to be able to pay for my order using a credit/debit card via Stripe so that I can complete my purchase securely.

## Background
The current checkout flow only supports PayPal. Business has requested Stripe as a primary payment method due to lower transaction fees (2.9% + $0.30 vs PayPal's 3.49% + $0.49).

## Technical Approach
- Integrate `Stripe.net` SDK (v43.x)
- Create `IPaymentGateway` interface to allow future provider swaps
- Store `PaymentIntentId` in `Orders` table for reconciliation
- Webhook endpoint at `/api/webhooks/stripe` for async event handling
- Support 3DS2 authentication flow

## Acceptance Criteria
- Given a customer enters valid card details, when they click Pay, then the order is created and payment is captured
- Given a card is declined, when Stripe returns a failure, then the user sees a clear error message and the order is not created
- Given a webhook arrives from Stripe, when the signature is valid, then the order status is updated accordingly
- Given the Stripe API is down, when a payment attempt fails, then a graceful fallback message is shown
acceptanceCriteria: Given a customer enters valid card details, when they click Pay, then the order is created and payment is captured
Given a card is declined, when Stripe returns a failure, then the user sees a clear error message and the order is not created
Given a webhook arrives from Stripe, when the signature is valid, then the order status is updated accordingly
Given the Stripe API is down, when a payment attempt fails, then a graceful fallback message is shown

comments.author: Alex Turner
comments.body: Started implementation. Created the IPaymentGateway interface and StripePaymentGateway implementation. Webhook endpoint is in progress.

comments.author: Sarah Mitchell
comments.body: Make sure the 3DS2 redirect flow is tested on mobile browsers — had issues with this in a previous project.

comments.author: Alex Turner
comments.body: Good point. Added mobile 3DS2 tests to the test plan. Using Stripe's test cards for the redirect flow.

subTasks.summary: Create IPaymentGateway interface and StripePaymentGateway implementation
Implement Stripe webhook endpoint with signature verification

SF-102._scenario: Bug — High priority, In Progress, detailed reproduction steps
SF-102.summary: Order total recalculated incorrectly when applying multiple discount codes
SF-102.description:
## Bug Report
When a customer applies two discount codes at checkout (e.g., a percentage discount + a fixed-amount discount), the final order total is calculated incorrectly. The percentage discount is applied to the post-fixed-discount price rather than the original price, resulting in a lower total than intended.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Add items totalling $100 to the cart
2. Apply discount code SAVE10 (10% off = $10 discount, total = $90)
3. Apply discount code FLAT5 (flat $5 off)
4. Expected: $90 - $5 = $85
5. Actual: $90 * 0.90 = $81 (system re-applies percentage discount to discounted price)

## Root Cause
Suspected issue in `DiscountService.ApplyDiscounts()` — discounts are applied sequentially without resetting base price.

## Impact
Revenue loss: ~$4 per affected order. Estimated 200-300 orders affected per day based on discount code usage logs.
acceptanceCriteria: Given multiple discount codes are applied, when order total is calculated, then each discount is applied to the original pre-discount price independently
Given a percentage and a flat discount are combined, when the total is shown, then the combined discount equals sum of both discounts applied to original price
Given the fix is deployed, when regression tests run, then all existing discount combination scenarios pass

comments.author: Priya Sharma
comments.body: Confirmed root cause in DiscountService.ApplyDiscounts(). The discounts list is iterated in order and each uses the running total rather than original price. Fix is straightforward.

comments.author: QA Team
comments.body: Also noticed this affects the order summary email — the breakdown shows wrong individual discount amounts.

SF-103._scenario: Task — To Do, medium priority, performance improvement
SF-103.summary: Add Redis caching layer for product catalogue API responses
SF-103.description:
The `/api/products` endpoint is queried ~50,000 times per hour during peak traffic. Product data changes at most once every 15 minutes (admin updates). Adding Redis caching with a 10-minute TTL will reduce database load by an estimated 90%.

## Approach
- Add `StackExchange.Redis` NuGet package
- Create `ICacheService` abstraction
- Wrap `ProductRepository.GetAllAsync()` with a cache-aside pattern
- Cache key: `products:all` and `products:{id}`
- TTL: 600 seconds (configurable via appsettings)
- Invalidate on product create/update/delete
acceptanceCriteria: Given a product list request hits the cache, when the TTL has not expired, then the response time is under 10ms
Given a product is updated, when the cache is invalidated, then the next request fetches fresh data from the database
Given Redis is unavailable, when a request comes in, then the system falls back to the database gracefully without throwing an exception

SF-201._scenario: Done ticket — eligible for User Guide generation
SF-201.summary: Add order tracking page with real-time status updates
SF-201.description: Customers can now track their order status in real-time via the /my-orders/{orderId}/track page. Status updates are pushed via SignalR every 30 seconds.
acceptanceCriteria: Given a customer navigates to the tracking page, when the page loads, then the current order status is displayed
Given an order status changes, when the SignalR update is received, then the page updates without a full refresh

comments.author: Emma Wilson
comments.body: Deployed to production on 2026-04-20. All acceptance criteria verified by QA.

SF-202._scenario: Done ticket — eligible for User Guide generation
SF-202.description: Customers can cancel an order within 30 minutes of placing it, as long as it has not entered the 'Shipped' status. The cancellation triggers an automatic refund via the original payment method.
acceptanceCriteria: Given an order was placed less than 30 minutes ago and is not yet shipped, when a customer requests cancellation, then the order is cancelled and a refund is initiated
Given an order was placed more than 30 minutes ago, when a customer attempts to cancel, then an appropriate error message is displayed
Given an order has status Shipped, when a customer attempts to cancel, then the cancel button is disabled

comments.author: QA Team
comments.body: Tested all edge cases including timezone boundary conditions. All pass.

SF-301_minimalFields._scenario: Edge case — ticket with minimal data (empty description, no comments, no AC)

SF-302_longDescription._scenario: Edge case — very long description, tests JiraDescriptionMaxChars truncation (currently 5000 chars)
SF-302_longDescription.summary: Migrate order database from SQL Server 2019 to Azure SQL Hyperscale
SF-302_longDescription.description:
## Overview
This is a major infrastructure migration ticket. The current SQL Server 2019 instance hosts the Orders database (1.2 TB, 480M rows in OrderItems). We are migrating to Azure SQL Hyperscale to enable auto-scaling read replicas and faster backup/restore times.

## Phase 1: Assessment
- Audit all stored procedures (47 identified, 12 use deprecated syntax)
- Profile slow queries (>500ms) — currently 23 identified
- Check collation compatibility (SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS → same on Azure SQL)
- Estimate downtime window: target <2 hours

## Phase 2: Schema Preparation
- Remove all deprecated NOLOCK hints from application queries
- Convert non-clustered columnstore indexes (not supported on Hyperscale)
- Update connection strings to use AAD authentication instead of SQL auth
- Add retry logic (Polly) for transient Azure SQL errors (error codes: 40613, 40501, 49918)

## Phase 3: Data Migration
- Use Azure Database Migration Service (DMS) for online migration
- Set up read replica in East US 2 as failover target
- Verify row counts and checksums post-migration
- Run parallel read test: 1000 concurrent queries on both old and new instance, compare response times

## Phase 4: Cutover
- Maintenance window: Saturday 2026-06-07 02:00 UTC
- Redirect connection strings via Azure App Configuration (no deployment needed)
- Monitor for 2 hours post-cutover
- Rollback plan: revert App Configuration value, RTO <5 minutes

## Phase 5: Decommission
- Keep SQL Server 2019 instance live for 2 weeks as safety net
- Decommission on 2026-06-21 if no issues reported

## Dependencies
- DevOps approval for maintenance window
- DBA sign-off on migration plan
- Azure DMS quota increase request (submitted 2026-04-30, ref: MSFT-QUOTA-88821)

## Risks
- Hyperscale does not support cross-database queries — identified 3 stored procs that need refactoring
- Azure SQL Hyperscale connection string format differs slightly — needs thorough app-side testing
- Cost increase: estimated $2,400/month additional for read replica (approved by Finance)

## Success Metrics
- Zero data loss post-migration
- P99 query latency improvement: target 40% reduction
- Auto-failover test: RTO < 30 seconds
acceptanceCriteria: Given the migration is complete, when row counts are verified, then Orders and OrderItems counts match exactly
Given Hyperscale is live, when 1000 concurrent queries run, then P99 latency is at least 40% lower than baseline
Given the primary node fails, when auto-failover triggers, then the read replica takes over within 30 seconds
Given the App Configuration value is reverted, when the rollback is executed, then all traffic returns to SQL Server 2019 within 5 minutes
